planting.note: Divide established clumps immediately after flowering in spring or in early fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, neutral to slightly acidic soil in shade; blue lungwort does not tolerate dry shade or drought. Unlike the common spotted lungwort (P. saccharata), this species has unspotted, plain-green, narrow leaves and is considered to have the most intensely blue flowers of any Pulmonaria.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, neutral to slightly acidic soil in part to full shade; blue lungwort is considered the finest species for flower color, producing the most intensely and purely cobalt-blue flowers of any Pulmonaria — a richer, deeper blue than the usually mauve-blue of common lungwort and superior in color purity to most named hybrids. The characteristic color-change from red-pink in bud to brilliant sky-blue as the flower opens is most dramatic in this species and gives mixed-color clusters a unique bicolor effect in early spring before any other shade perennials have emerged. Unlike the familiar spotted lungwort (P. saccharata) and its many hybrids, the leaves of P. angustifolia are plain, unspotted, medium-green, and narrower, making it distinctly different in appearance throughout the growing season. It forms a compact, low groundcover of attractive foliage that persists through summer into fall in moist shade conditions. Combined with early daffodils and hellebores, it creates one of the most effective early-spring shade-garden combinations.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early fall or immediately after flowering in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade; long-leaved lungwort is notably more heat- and drought-tolerant than other Pulmonaria species, which is significant for gardeners in zones 6-8 where summer heat limits most lungworts. The cultivar Bertram Anderson has exceptionally heavy silver spotting on very long, narrow leaves and is the most widely grown selection.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; long-leaved lungwort is the most distinct species in the genus, immediately recognizable by its extraordinarily long, very narrow, lance-shaped leaves that can reach 20-24 inches in length — far longer than the broader, more rounded leaves of other Pulmonaria species. These elongated leaves are heavily marked with irregular silver-white spots and splashes that cover much of the leaf surface, particularly in the cultivar Bertram Anderson which has some of the most heavily spotted leaves of any shade perennial. The late-winter and early-spring flowers are deep violet-blue to blue-violet, produced in drooping cymes on 8-12 inch stems. Compared to other lungworts, P. longifolia shows better heat and drought tolerance during summer, making it a better performer in warmer parts of the range. The distinctive long, spotted leaves provide an elegant, sophisticated texture in the shade garden throughout the growing season.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early fall or plant container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, neutral to slightly acidic soil in part to full shade; Cappadocian navelwort performs best in dappled or half shade with consistent moisture. The cultivar Cherry Ingram has slightly larger, more intensely blue flowers; Starry Eyes has distinctive blue-and-white bicolor petals. It is more long-lived than the spring-flowering O. verna.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, neutral to slightly acidic soil in part to full shade; Cappadocian navelwort is a low-growing shade perennial from Turkey producing small, perfect, pure sky-blue flowers with a white eye — identical in form to forget-me-not flowers but more intensely colored, longer-blooming, and on a more reliably perennial plant. The flowers appear in early to mid spring in loose sprays above neat mounds of dark-green, heart-shaped, somewhat hairy leaves and have a particularly clear, clean blue that is outstanding in the shade garden. Unlike the more common spring navelwort (O. verna), which dies back and goes dormant in summer, O. cappadocica is reliably evergreen or semi-evergreen and maintains its attractive foliage through the growing season as a groundcover. The cultivar Cherry Ingram is the most floriferous selection; Starry Eyes has bicolored flowers with a blue center and white picotee edge to the petals — a genuinely beautiful and unusual flower form. An outstanding companion for hellebores, epimediums, and ferns in the woodland garden.

planting.note: Sow seeds directly in well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, moderately fertile soil in full sun in early spring or fall; Italian bugloss is tap-rooted and resents transplanting once established, so direct seeding or planting very small container plants is preferable. Cut back hard after the first flush of bloom in June to encourage a second flowering in late summer. Short-lived in rich, wet soils; thrives in lean, well-drained conditions.

directions: Sow seeds in well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Italian bugloss produces flowers of a true, pure, brilliant blue that is almost unmatched in the late-spring perennial garden — the open, five-petaled, white-eyed flowers are produced in large, branching clusters on tall, leafy stems and are among the most intensely blue of any hardy perennial. As a member of the borage family, it is exceptionally attractive to bees and butterflies throughout its long June-July bloom period, and cutting back the spent flower stalks after the first flush often promotes a secondary flush in late summer. The plant is coarse and hairy in texture — the broad, rough leaves and stems have bristly hairs that can cause skin irritation in sensitive individuals — and the tap root makes division impractical, but these characteristics matter little given the breathtaking blue of the flowers. Best planted in the middle or back of a sunny border where the coarser foliage is hidden by neighboring plants. The cultivar Loddon Royalist is the most floriferous and most widely grown selection.

planting.note: Sow seeds directly in fall for spring germination, or sow in early spring; viper's bugloss is a biennial that germinates and forms a basal rosette in year one, then flowers in year two. It self-sows prolifically in dry, sunny, well-drained conditions to maintain a permanent colony. It thrives in sandy, gravelly, chalky, or lean soils and actually blooms more abundantly in poor, dry soils than in rich ones.

directions: Sow seeds in lean, well-drained, alkaline to neutral, sandy or chalky soil in full sun; viper's bugloss is one of the supreme bee and butterfly plants of European wildflower meadows — a bristly, rough-textured biennial covered from late spring through midsummer with brilliant, vivid blue (occasionally pink or white) flowers that are produced in extraordinary profusion from the curving, scorpioid flower clusters characteristic of the borage family. In a good year a single plant produces hundreds of flowers over a period of months, each flower visited repeatedly by bumblebees, honeybees, and solitary bees. The name comes from the snake-like shape of the nutlets (seeds) which resemble a snake's head. Native to dry, sunny, chalky grasslands and disturbed ground throughout Europe, it thrives in the lean, alkaline, well-drained soils of roadsides, banks, and gravel gardens where it self-sows freely to maintain a permanent colony. Note: while naturalized across much of North America, it is classed as a noxious weed in some western states due to toxicity to livestock from pyrrolizidine alkaloids.
